1: Observe the month of Abib and celebrate the Passover — "for in the month of Abib the LORD your God brought you out of Egypt by night." The Passover feast commands annual re-enactment of the foundational redemption. Paul declares the Christological fulfillment: "Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed" (1 Corinthians 5:7). Jesus eats the Passover with his disciples and transforms it into the Lord's Supper (Luke 22:14–20), the meal that re-enacts not the Exodus from Egypt but the greater exodus of death and resurrection. Direct type.
2: "You shall sacrifice the Passover offering to the LORD your God — from the flock or the herd." The Passover offering from the flock is the lamb; the lamb is the symbol that runs through the entire biblical witness to Christ: John the Baptist ("Behold, the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world", John 1:29), Paul (1 Corinthians 5:7), Peter (1 Peter 1:19), and Revelation (the Lamb on the throne, Revelation 5:6). The Passover lamb commanded in Deuteronomy is the type whose sacrifice defines the whole. Direct type.
3: "For seven days you shall eat unleavened bread — the bread of affliction — because you left Egypt in haste." The unleavened bread is the bread of urgency and affliction — the opposite of the leisured leavened bread of ordinary life. Paul extends the unleavened-bread metaphor directly: "Cleanse out the old leaven that you may be a new lump, as you really are unleavened. For Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed. Let us therefore celebrate the festival, not with the old leaven... but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth" (1 Corinthians 5:7–8). Direct NT citation.
4: "None of the flesh you sacrifice on the evening of the first day shall remain until morning." The Passover lamb is fully consumed; nothing is left to decay. The Passover regulations anticipate Christ's body: "not a bone of him shall be broken" (John 19:36, citing Exodus 12:46) — the regulations about the Passover lamb are fulfilled in the manner of Christ's death. And his body does not decay (Acts 2:31, citing Psalms 16:10 — "nor will you let your Holy One see corruption"). Direct type.
5: The Passover may not be sacrificed in just any town — only at the central sanctuary. The centralization of the Passover sacrifice at the one place YHWH chooses. In fulfillment: Jesus is crucified in Jerusalem, the central sanctuary city, during the Passover feast. John's Gospel especially emphasizes the temporal and geographic precision (John 18:28; Deuteronomy 19:14, 31) — the Passover lamb is slain in exactly the place and at exactly the time the law required. Direct fulfillment.
6: "In the evening, at sunset, at the very time you came out of Egypt." The Passover sacrifice must occur at the specific time of the original Exodus. The sacrifice is temporally tethered to its founding event. Jesus is crucified during the Passover, as the Passover lambs are being slaughtered in the temple (John 19:14 notes it is "about noon on the day of Preparation for the Passover" — the afternoon when lambs were slaughtered). The typological precision is exact. Direct fulfillment.
7: "Cook and eat it at the place the LORD your God will choose, and in the morning you shall return to your tents." The Passover meal eaten at the central sanctuary, then return to ordinary life. The morning after the Passover sacrifice is the morning of freedom — the original Exodus began in the morning after the night of the angel of death. In Christ's Passover, the morning after is Easter morning — "On the first day of the week, at early dawn, they went to the tomb" (Luke 24:1). The Passover morning becomes the resurrection morning. Type.
8: "On the seventh day there shall be a solemn assembly for the LORD your God — you shall do no work on that day." The seventh day closes the Passover week with a Sabbath assembly. The Sabbath-rest structure of the feast week points toward the eschatological rest that Christ inaugurates. Hebrews 4:9–10 — "there remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God, for whoever has entered God's rest has also rested from his works as God did from his" — is the Christological fulfillment of the Sabbath assembly that closes the Passover week. Theme.
9: "Count off seven weeks from when the sickle is first put to the standing grain." The Feast of Weeks (Shavuot/Pentecost) begins with the barley harvest and is counted from Passover. The Spirit is poured out on the church at Pentecost (Acts 2:1–4) — the Feast of Weeks, the harvest festival. The Spirit is the firstfruits of the eschatological harvest (Romans 8:23). The harvest-timing of the Feast of Weeks is the shadow; Pentecost as the harvest of the Spirit's first fruits is the fulfillment. Direct fulfillment (Acts 2).
10: "Celebrate the Feast of Weeks to the LORD your God with a freewill offering — give in proportion to how the LORD your God has blessed you." The Feast of Weeks is a freewill offering feast — proportional, generous, responsive to blessing. In Acts 2, the Spirit poured out at Pentecost produces exactly this generosity: "All who believed were together and had all things in common. And they were selling their possessions and belongings and distributing the proceeds to all" (Acts 2:44–45). The freewill-offering feast becomes the Spirit-generated generosity of the community. Type.
11: "You shall rejoice before the LORD your God — you, your son and daughter, your servants, the Levite, the sojourner, the fatherless, and the widow." The Feast of Weeks is comprehensively inclusive — the social hierarchy is irrelevant in the joy before YHWH. In Christ, this inclusivity is radicalized: "There is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free, there is no male and female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus" (Galatians 3:28). The feast-inclusion of the vulnerable and the foreigner becomes, in Christ, the abolition of every division. Theme.
12: "Remember that you were a slave in Egypt, and be careful to observe these statutes." Memory of slavery grounds festival observance — the feast is joyful precisely because slavery is over. In the Lord's Supper, the memory of spiritual slavery — "do this in remembrance of me" (Luke 22:19) — grounds the eucharistic joy. The freed slave's feast and the redeemed sinner's eucharist share the same logic: joy rooted in remembered deliverance. Theme.
13: The Feast of Booths (Sukkot) — seven days at the final harvest, when all the produce has been gathered. Sukkot is the feast that commemorates Israel's wilderness dwelling in booths. Jesus is in Jerusalem for the Feast of Booths in John 7–8 — the backdrop for his most explicit claims about himself as the source of living water (John 7:37–38) and as the light of the world (John 8:12). His presence at Sukkot activates the feast's imagery: he is what the water-pouring ceremony and the lamp-lighting ceremony at Sukkot pointed toward. Shadow.
14: Rejoice at the Feast of Booths — all of you together, including servants, Levites, sojourners, fatherless, and widows. The comprehensive joy of Sukkot. Zechariah 14:16 envisions all nations coming up to Jerusalem to celebrate the Feast of Booths in the messianic age — the feast that was once Israel's becomes the feast of all nations. In Christ, the eschatological Sukkot begins: "Salvation belongs to our God who sits on the throne, and to the Lamb!" (Revelation 7:10) — the great multitude from all nations celebrates the ultimate harvest feast. Theme.
15: "The LORD your God will bless you in all your produce and in all the work of your hands; and you shall be completely joyful." Complete joy — the fullness of covenant blessing expressed as total festive delight. Jesus promises this same fullness: "I have said these things to you, that my joy may be in you, and that your joy may be full" (John 15:11). The complete joy of Sukkot is the OT anticipation of the fullness of joy that Christ gives. Theme.
16: "Three times a year all your males shall appear before the LORD your God at the place he will choose: at the Feast of Unleavened Bread, at the Feast of Weeks, and at the Feast of Booths." The three pilgrimage feasts as the covenant calendar's framework. All three are Christologically fulfilled: Passover/Unleavened Bread in Christ's death and resurrection, the Feast of Weeks/Pentecost in the Spirit's outpouring, and the Feast of Booths in Christ's tabernacling with his people (John 1:14 — "the Word became flesh and tabernacled among us," ἐσκήνωσεν). The entire covenant calendar finds its fulfillment in Christ. Theme becoming fulfillment.
17: "Every man shall give as he is able, in proportion to the blessing the LORD your God has given him." Giving proportional to received blessing — the festival offering is calibrated to what YHWH has done. In Christ, the measure of giving is redefined by the measure of God's giving: "For you know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ, that though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you by his poverty might become rich" (2 Corinthians 8:9). The standard of proportional giving is set by the cross. Revelation of God.
18: "You shall appoint judges and officials throughout all your towns... and they shall judge the people with just decisions." The covenant community requires just judgment at every level. Christ as the just judge fulfills what the covenant's judicial system could only approximate: "the Father... has given all judgment to the Son" (John 5:22); "he has fixed a day on which he will judge the world in righteousness by a man whom he has appointed" (Acts 17:31). The local judges are the shadow; the universal Judge is Christ. Shadow.
19: "Do not pervert justice; do not show favoritism; do not accept a bribe — for a bribe blinds the eyes of the wise and twists the words of the innocent." Three prohibitions that protect the innocent. Judas' acceptance of thirty pieces of silver (Matthew 26:14–16) is the precise inversion: a bribe accepted to twist the words and destroy the innocent one. The judicial standard of Deuteronomy is violated in the very act that fulfills the Passover type — the condemnation of the one perfectly innocent man becomes the sacrifice that atones. Theme/irony.
20: "Justice, and justice alone, you shall pursue — so that you may live and possess the land." The double emphasis on justice — ṣeḏeq ṣeḏeq — the repetition signaling absolute commitment. In Christ, divine justice and divine mercy meet at the cross: "God presented Christ as a propitiation through faith in his blood... to demonstrate his righteousness... so that he would be just and the one who justifies those who have faith in Jesus" (Romans 3:25–26). The justice pursued in Deuteronomy is realized in the cross. Revelation of God.
21: "You shall not set up any wooden Asherah pole beside the altar of the LORD your God." The prohibition of syncretism — YHWH's altar must not be mixed with Canaanite fertility symbols. The jealous exclusivity of YHWH's worship is the OT ground for Paul's: "You cannot drink the cup of the Lord and the cup of demons. You cannot partake of the table of the Lord and the table of demons" (1 Corinthians 10:21). In Christ, the exclusive devotion demanded here is the devotion to the one who is himself the way, the truth, and the life — no other pole alongside him. Theme.
22: "Do not erect a sacred stone pillar, for the LORD your God hates these." The maṣṣēḇâ (standing stone) was a Canaanite fertility symbol. YHWH's hatred of these idols is the expression of his exclusive claim on Israel's worship. In Christ, the exclusive claim is made in personal terms: "No one comes to the Father except through me" (John 14:6). The prohibition of substitute worship is fulfilled in the person who is himself the only legitimate approach to God. Revelation of God.
